summary: Gruber pushes back against recurring narratives that the Apple Watch is a failure because it hasn't matched iPhone-level revenue. He notes these stories appear like clockwork before every earnings report and rely on the flawed premise that only iPhone-sized businesses count as successes. He points out that Apple Watch outsold every watch company except Rolex by revenue in 2015, and that Apple's 'Other' category had a near-record holiday quarter. He argues that judging every product against the most successful consumer product in history is an unreasonable standard that would paralyze any company.
key_points: Apple deliberately chose not to report Watch sales numbers for competitive reasons, which journalists repeatedly treat as evidence of failure
The iPhone is the most profitable consumer product in history, making it an absurd benchmark for every other product
Apple Watch outsold every traditional watch company except Rolex by revenue, which by any normal standard is a hit
If Apple only pursued iPhone-sized opportunities, it would paralyze the company — singles matter while waiting for the next home run
money_quotes: These articles come out like clockwork every 3 months, as Apple's earnings report draws near.
The nut of every 'Apple Watch is a dud' story is the fact that it's clearly not an iPhone-size business. But that can't be the only measure of success.
The iPhone is the biggest and most successful consumer product in the history of the world.
You and I may never again see a product as profitable as the iPhone — not just from Apple, but from any company in any industry.
In baseball terms, it's fine for Apple to hit a bunch of singles while waiting for their next home run.
These two facts are both true: Apple Watch sales are a rounding error compared to the iPhone, and Apple Watch is a smash hit compared to traditional watches and other wearable devices.
key_insight: Measuring every Apple product against the iPhone — the most profitable consumer product in history — guarantees that everything else looks like a failure, even products that dominate their actual markets.
